# The biological applications of DNA nanomaterials: current challenges and future directions

[*Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy*](/sigtrans)
**volume 6**, Article number: 351 (2021)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

47k Accesses

251 Citations

11 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

DNA, a genetic material, has been employed in different scientific directions for various biological applications as driven by DNA nanotechnology in the past decades, including tissue regeneration, disease prevention, inflammation inhibition, bioimaging, biosensing, diagnosis, antitumor drug delivery, and therapeutics. With the rapid progress in DNA nanotechnology, multitudinous DNA nanomaterials have been designed with different shape and size based on the classic Watson–Crick base-pairing for molecular self-assembly. Some DNA materials could functionally change cell biological behaviors, such as cell migration, cell proliferation, cell differentiation, autophagy, and anti-inflammatory effects. Some single-stranded DNAs (ssDNAs) or RNAs with secondary structures via self-pairing, named aptamer, possess the ability of targeting, which are selected by systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) and applied for tumor targeted diagnosis and treatment. Some DNA nanomaterials with three-dimensional (3D) nanostructures and stable structures are investigated as drug carrier systems to delivery multiple antitumor medicine or gene therapeutic agents. While the functional DNA nanostructures have promoted the development of the DNA nanotechnology with innovative designs and preparation strategies, and also proved with great potential in the biological and medical use, there is still a long way to go for the eventual application of DNA materials in real life. Here in this review, we conducted a comprehensive survey of the structural development history of various DNA nanomaterials, introduced the principles of different DNA nanomaterials, summarized their biological applications in different fields, and discussed the current challenges and further directions that could help to achieve their applications in the future.

## Introduction

DNA was used for the storage of genetic information as a common biomolecule and fundamental hereditary material in different creatures.[1](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR1 "Dong, Y. et al. DNA functional materials assembled from branched DNA: design, synthesis, and applications. Chem. Rev. 120, 9420–9481 (2020)."),[2](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR2 "Broker, T. R. & Lehman, I. R. Branched DNA molecules: intermediates in T4 recombination. J. Mol. Biol. 60, 131–149 (1971).") DNA materials possess the potential for nanofabrication due to its characteristic nature, i.e., high precision of Watson–Crick base-pairing and high controllability.[1](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR1 "Dong, Y. et al. DNA functional materials assembled from branched DNA: design, synthesis, and applications. Chem. Rev. 120, 9420–9481 (2020)."),[3](#ref-CR3 "Seeman, N. C. Struct. 27, 225–248 (1998).") proposed the rules for the synthesis of DNA nanostructures for the first time, which triggered a booming years of DNA nanotechnology development.[6](#ref-CR6 "Seeman, N. C. Key experimental approaches in DNA nanotechnology. Curr. Protoc. Nucleic Acid Chem. Chapter 12, 11 (2002)."),[10](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR10 "Fan, S. et al. Create nanoscale patterns with DNA origami. Small 15, e1805554 (2019).") In the following decades, the technology of DNA nanostructures developed and improved rapidly, and various DNA nanostructures have been designed and widely applied as generic polymeric materials to form complex nanoparticles for specific usages in the fields of chemistry and biomaterial.[11](#ref-CR11 "Jones, M. R., Seeman, N. C. & Mirkin, C. A. Nanomaterials. programmable materials and the nature of the DNA bond. Science 347, 1260901 (2015)."),[12](#ref-CR12 "Hu, Y. & Niemeyer, C. M. Engl. 56, 15210–15233 (2017)."),[16](#ref-CR16 "Seeman, N. C. DNA in a material world. Nature 421, 427–431 (2003)."),[17](#ref-CR17 "Jia, S. et al. Engineering and mapping nanocavity emission via precision placement of DNA origami. Nature 535, 401–405 (2016).")

DNA, the carrier and transmitter of genetic information in all living systems, consists of four different deoxynucleotide monomers.[5](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR5 "Seeman, N. C. & Sleiman, H. F. DNA nanotechnology. Nat. Rev. Mater 3, 17068 (2018).") Each monomer is formed with a phosphate group, a deoxyribose, and one of four nitrogen-involving nucleobases, while the nucleobases include thymine (T), adenine (A), guanine (G), and cytosine (C).[1](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR1 "Dong, Y. et al. DNA functional materials assembled from branched DNA: design, synthesis, and applications. Chem. Rev. 120, 9420–9481 (2020)."),[19](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR19 "Wing, R. et al. Crystal structure analysis of a complete turn of B-DNA. Nature 287, 755–758 (1980).") Between the monomers, the phosphate group and the deoxyribose can form the phosphodiester bond, which links a certain number of monomers into a DNA chain, named single-stranded DNA (ssDNA).[1](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR1 "Dong, Y. et al. DNA functional materials assembled from branched DNA: design, synthesis, and applications. Chem. Rev. 120, 9420–9481 (2020)."),[19](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR19 "Wing, R. et al. Crystal structure analysis of a complete turn of B-DNA. Nature 287, 755–758 (1980).") Based on the principle of Watson–Crick base-pairing, the continuous ssDNA can pair with another one in antiparallel directions to form a double-helix DNA structure, that is double-stranded DNA (dsDNA). Between the two polymeric chains, the nucleobases of C and G can form three hydrogen bonds, and A and T can form two hydrogen bonds, resulting in the base pairs with the certain complementary ssDNA inside the dsDNA.[20](#ref-CR20 "Franklin, R. E. & Gosling, R. G. Molecular structure of nucleic acids. Molecular configuration in sodium thymonucleate. 1953. Ann. N. Y. Acad. Sci. 758, 16–17 (1995)."),[21](#ref-CR21 "Franklin, R. E. & Gosling, R. G. Molecular configuration in sodium thymonucleate. Nature 171, 740–741 (1953)."),[22](#ref-CR22 "Wilkins, M. H., Stokes, A. R. & Wilson, H. R. Molecular structure of nucleic acids. Molecular structure of deoxypentose nucleic acids. 1953. Ann. N. Y. Acad. Sci. 758, 13–16 (1995)."),[23](#ref-CR23 "Wilkins, M. H., Stokes, A. R. & Wilson, H. R. Molecular structure of deoxypentose nucleic acids. Nature 171, 738–740 (1953)."),[24](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR24 "Watson, J. D. & Crick, F. H. Molecular structure of nucleic acids; a structure for deoxyribose nucleic acid. Nature 171, 737–738 (1953).")

Despite that DNA molecule is composed of simple units, the variety of dsDNA and the programmability of the structure can be realized by accurate design and freely defined constitutions. In artificially synthesized dsDNAs, the quantity and order of the four monomers are accurately arranged as designed, which determines the diversity of dsDNAs. The freely defined constitutions of DNA are based on sequence-specific base attacking interactions, which are determined by both the external conditions and the sequences of DNA duplexes.[25](#ref-CR25 "Mergny, J. DNA nanomaterials were developed with a wide variety of structures, including two- and three-dimension (2D and 3D) constructions. Furthermore, according to the various molecular building-styles of functional DNA materials, the constructions of artificially synthesized DNA nanostructures contain single-layer and multilayer nanostructures, which also can be sorted into linear, circular, and branched form.[57](/articles/s41392-021-00727-9#ref-CR57 "Roh, Y. H., Ruiz, R. C., Peng, S., Lee, J. B. & Luo, D. Engineering DNA-based functional materials. Chem. Soc. Rev. 40, 5730–5744 (2011).") The DNA origami technique makes it easy for preparation of DNA, which also promotes the explosive development of structural DNA nanotechnology. The advanced development of DNA nanotechnology then breeds various novel DNA nanomaterials that are applied widely, including tissue engineering, immune engineering, drug delivering, disease diagnosis, and as tools for molecular biology or as biosensors.[54](#ref-CR54 "Li, J. et al. SUMMARY:
DNA nanotechnology is the design and manufacture of artificial nucleic acid structures for technological uses.

FULL CONTENT:
![](/static/images/icons/wikipedia.png)

## Contents

# DNA nanotechnology

![Featured article](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/e7/Cscr-featured.svg/20px-Cscr-featured.svg.png)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Supramolecular_Assembly_Lehn.jpg/120px-Supramolecular_Assembly_Lehn.jpg)
![icon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Nuvola_apps_kalzium.svg/40px-Nuvola_apps_kalzium.svg.png)
![icon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Noun-technology.svg/40px-Noun-technology.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/DNA_tetrahedron_white.png/330px-DNA_tetrahedron_white.png)

**DNA nanotechnology** is the design and manufacture of artificial [nucleic acid](/wiki/Nucleic_acid "Nucleic acid") structures for technological uses. In this field, nucleic acids are used as non-biological engineering materials for [nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology") rather than as the carriers of genetic information in living [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)"). Researchers in the field have created static structures such as two- and three-dimensional [crystal lattices](/wiki/Crystal_structure "Crystal structure"), [nanotubes](/wiki/Nanotube "Nanotube"), [polyhedra](/wiki/Polyhedron "Polyhedron"), and arbitrary shapes, and functional devices such as [molecular machines](/wiki/Molecular_machine "Molecular machine") and [DNA computers](/wiki/DNA_computing "DNA computing"). The field is beginning to be used as a tool to solve [basic science](/wiki/Basic_science "Basic science") problems in [structural biology](/wiki/Structural_biology "Structural biology") and [biophysics](/wiki/Biophysics "Biophysics"), including applications in [X-ray crystallography](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography") and [nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy of proteins](/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance_spectroscopy_of_proteins "Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy of proteins") to determine structures. Potential applications in [molecular scale electronics](/wiki/Molecular_scale_electronics "Molecular scale electronics") and [nanomedicine](/wiki/Nanomedicine "Nanomedicine") are also being investigated.

The conceptual foundation for DNA nanotechnology was first laid out by [Nadrian Seeman](/wiki/Nadrian_Seeman "Nadrian Seeman") in the early 1980s, and the field began to attract widespread interest in the mid-2000s. This use of nucleic acids is enabled by their strict [base pairing](/wiki/Base_pair "Base pair") rules, which cause only portions of strands with [complementary](/wiki/Complementarity_(molecular_biology) "Complementarity (molecular biology)") [base sequences](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_sequence "Nucleic acid sequence") to bind together to form strong, rigid [double helix](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_double_helix "Nucleic acid double helix") structures. This allows for the [rational design of base sequences](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_design "Nucleic acid design") that will selectively assemble to form complex target structures with precisely controlled [nanoscale](/wiki/Nanoscopic_scale "Nanoscopic scale") features. Several assembly methods are used to make these structures, including tile-based structures that assemble from smaller structures, folding structures using the [DNA origami](/wiki/DNA_origami "DNA origami") method, and dynamically reconfigurable structures using strand displacement methods. The field's name specifically references [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA"), but the same principles have been used with other types of nucleic acids as well, leading to the occasional use of the alternative name *nucleic acid nanotechnology*.

## History

The conceptual foundation for DNA nanotechnology was first laid out by [Nadrian Seeman](/wiki/Nadrian_Seeman "Nadrian Seeman") in the early 1980s.[[2]](#cite_note-Pelesko-2) Seeman's original motivation was to create a three-dimensional DNA lattice for orienting other large molecules, which would simplify their [crystallographic study](/wiki/Crystallography "Crystallography") by eliminating the difficult process of obtaining pure crystals. This idea had reportedly come to him in late 1980, after realizing the similarity between the woodcut *Depth* by [M. C. Escher](/wiki/M._C._Escher "M. C. Escher") and an array of DNA six-arm junctions.[[3]](#cite_note-Seeman-sciam-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Several natural branched DNA structures were known at the time, including the DNA [replication fork](/wiki/Replication_fork "Replication fork") and the mobile [Holliday junction](/wiki/Holliday_junction "Holliday junction"), but Seeman's insight was that immobile nucleic acid junctions could be created by properly designing the strand sequences to remove symmetry in the assembled molecule, and that these immobile junctions could in principle be combined into rigid crystalline lattices. The first theoretical paper proposing this scheme was published in 1982, and the first experimental demonstration of an immobile DNA junction was published the following year.[[5]](#cite_note-Seeman2010-5)[[6]](#cite_note-ShihYanChallenges-6)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/7d/Escher_Depth.jpg/250px-Escher_Depth.jpg)

In 1991, Seeman's laboratory published a report on the synthesis of a cube made of DNA, the first synthetic three-dimensional nucleic acid nanostructure, for which he received the 1995 [Feynman Prize in Nanotechnology](/wiki/Foresight_Institute_Feynman_Prize_in_Nanotechnology "Foresight Institute Feynman Prize in Nanotechnology"). This was followed by a DNA [truncated octahedron](/wiki/Truncated_octahedron "Truncated octahedron"). It soon became clear that these structures, polygonal shapes with flexible junctions as their [vertices](/wiki/Vertex_(geometry) "Vertex (geometry)"), were not rigid enough to form extended three-dimensional lattices. Seeman developed the more rigid double-crossover (DX) [structural motif](/wiki/Structural_motif "Structural motif"), and in 1998, in collaboration with [Erik Winfree](/wiki/Erik_Winfree "Erik Winfree"), published the creation of two-dimensional lattices of DX tiles.[[3]](#cite_note-Seeman-sciam-3)[[2]](#cite_note-Pelesko-2)[[7]](#cite_note-origamichapter-7) These tile-based structures had the advantage that they provided the ability to implement DNA computing, which was demonstrated by Winfree and [Paul Rothemund](/wiki/Paul_Rothemund "Paul Rothemund") in their 2004 paper on the algorithmic self-assembly of a Sierpinski gasket structure, and for which they shared the 2006 Feynman Prize in Nanotechnology. Winfree's key insight was that the DX tiles could be used as [Wang tiles](/wiki/Wang_tile "Wang tile"), meaning that their assembly could perform computation.[[2]](#cite_note-Pelesko-2) The synthesis of a three-dimensional lattice was finally published by Seeman in 2009, nearly thirty years after he had set out to achieve it.[[8]](#cite_note-growsup-8)

New abilities continued to be discovered for designed DNA structures throughout the 2000s. The first [DNA nanomachine](/wiki/DNA_machine "DNA machine")—a motif that changes its structure in response to an input—was demonstrated in 1999 by Seeman. An improved system, which was the first nucleic acid device to make use of toehold-mediated strand displacement, was demonstrated by [Bernard Yurke](/wiki/Bernard_Yurke "Bernard Yurke") in 2000.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The next advance was to translate this into mechanical motion, and in 2004 and 2005, several DNA walker systems were demonstrated by the groups of Seeman, [Niles Pierce](/wiki/Niles_Pierce "Niles Pierce"), [Andrew Turberfield](/wiki/Andrew_Turberfield "Andrew Turberfield"), and [Chengde Mao](/w/index.php?title=Chengde_Mao&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chengde Mao (page does not exist)").[[10]](#cite_note-nanomachines-10) The idea of using DNA arrays to template the assembly of other molecules such as nanoparticles and proteins, first suggested by Bruche Robinson and Seeman in 1987,[[11]](#cite_note-biochip-11) was demonstrated in 2002 by Seeman, Kiehl et al.[[12]](#cite_note-Nanoparticles_on_DNA-12) and subsequently by many other groups.

In 2006, Rothemund first demonstrated the [DNA origami](/wiki/DNA_origami "DNA origami") method for easily and robustly forming folded DNA structures of arbitrary shape. Rothemund had conceived of this method as being conceptually intermediate between Seeman's DX lattices, which used many short strands, and [William Shih](/w/index.php?title=William_Shih&action=edit&redlink=1 "William Shih (page does not exist)")'s DNA octahedron, which consisted mostly of one very long strand. Rothemund's DNA origami contains a long strand which folding is assisted by several short strands. This method allowed forming much larger structures than formerly possible, and which are less technically demanding to design and synthesize.[[7]](#cite_note-origamichapter-7) DNA origami was the cover story of *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")* on March 15, 2006.[[13]](#cite_note-rothemundorigami-13) Rothemund's research demonstrating two-dimensional DNA origami structures was followed by the demonstration of solid three-dimensional DNA origami by Douglas *et al.* in 2009,[[14]](#cite_note-origami-14) while the labs of [Jørgen Kjems](/w/index.php?title=J%C3%B8rgen_Kjems&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jørgen Kjems (page does not exist)") and Yan demonstrated hollow three-dimensional structures made out of two-dimensional faces.[[8]](#cite_note-growsup-8)

DNA nanotechnology was initially met with some skepticism due to the unusual non-biological use of nucleic acids as materials for building structures and doing computation, and the preponderance of [proof of principle](/wiki/Proof_of_principle "Proof of principle") experiments that extended the abilities of the field but were far from actual applications. Seeman's 1991 paper on the synthesis of the DNA cube was rejected by the journal *[Science](/wiki/Science_(journal) "Science (journal)")* after one reviewer praised its originality while another criticized it for its lack of biological relevance.[[15]](#cite_note-15) By the early 2010s the field was considered to have increased its abilities to the point that applications for basic science research were beginning to be realized, and practical applications in medicine and other fields were beginning to be considered feasible.[[8]](#cite_note-growsup-8)[[16]](#cite_note-16) The field had grown from very few active laboratories in 2001 to at least 60 in 2010, which increased the talent pool and thus the number of scientific advances in the field during that decade.[[17]](#cite_note-SeemanGrowing-17)

## Fundamental concepts

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/%D0%9D%D0%B5%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D1%81%D1%82%D1%80%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%82%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B0_%D0%A5%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%8F_%28%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B3%D0%BB.%29.svg/250px-%D0%9D%D0%B5%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B6%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D1%81%D1%82%D1%80%D1%83%D0%BA%D1%82%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B0_%D0%A5%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%BB%D0%B8%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%8F_%28%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B3%D0%BB.%29.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Mao-DX-schematic-2.svg/250px-Mao-DX-schematic-2.svg.png)

### Properties of nucleic acids

[Nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology") is often defined as the study of materials and devices with features on a scale below 100 [nanometers](/wiki/Nanometer "Nanometer"). DNA nanotechnology, specifically, is an example of [bottom-up](/wiki/Top-down_and_bottom-up_design "Top-down and bottom-up design") [molecular self-assembly](/wiki/Molecular_self-assembly "Molecular self-assembly"), in which molecular components spontaneously organize into stable structures; the particular form of these structures is induced by the physical and chemical properties of the components selected by the designers.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In DNA nanotechnology, the component materials are strands of nucleic acids such as DNA; these strands are often synthetic and are almost always used outside the context of a living cell. DNA is well-suited to nanoscale construction because the binding between two nucleic acid strands depends on simple [base pairing](/wiki/Base_pair "Base pair") rules which are well understood, and form the specific nanoscale structure of the [nucleic acid double helix](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_double_helix "Nucleic acid double helix"). These qualities make the assembly of nucleic acid structures easy to control through [nucleic acid design](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_design "Nucleic acid design"). This property is absent in other materials used in nanotechnology, including [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein"), for which [protein design](/wiki/Protein_design "Protein design") is very difficult, and [nanoparticles](/wiki/Nanoparticle "Nanoparticle"), which lack the capability for specific assembly on their own.[[5]](#cite_note-Seeman2010-5)

The [structure](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_structure "Nucleic acid structure") of a nucleic acid molecule consists of a sequence of [nucleotides](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide") distinguished by which [nucleobase](/wiki/Nucleobase "Nucleobase") they contain. In DNA, the four bases present are [adenine](/wiki/Adenine "Adenine") (A), [cytosine](/wiki/Cytosine "Cytosine") (C), [guanine](/wiki/Guanine "Guanine") (G), and [thymine](/wiki/Thymine "Thymine") (T). Nucleic acids have the property that two molecules will only bind to each other to form a double helix if the two sequences are [complementary](/wiki/Complementarity_(molecular_biology) "Complementarity (molecular biology)"), meaning that they form matching sequences of base pairs, with A only binding to T, and C only to G.[[5]](#cite_note-Seeman2010-5)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Because the formation of correctly matched base pairs is [energetically favorable](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_thermodynamics "Nucleic acid thermodynamics"), nucleic acid strands are expected in most cases to bind to each other in the conformation that maximizes the number of correctly paired bases. The sequences of bases in a system of strands thus determine the pattern of binding and the overall structure in an easily controllable way. In DNA nanotechnology, the base sequences of strands are rationally designed by researchers so that the base pairing interactions cause the strands to assemble in the desired conformation.[[3]](#cite_note-Seeman-sciam-3)[[5]](#cite_note-Seeman2010-5) While [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") is the dominant material used, structures incorporating other nucleic acids such as [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA") and [peptide nucleic acid](/wiki/Peptide_nucleic_acid "Peptide nucleic acid") (PNA) have also been constructed.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Subfields

DNA nanotechnology is sometimes divided into two overlapping subfields: structural DNA nanotechnology and dynamic DNA nanotechnology. Structural DNA nanotechnology, sometimes abbreviated as SDN, focuses on synthesizing and characterizing nucleic acid complexes and materials that assemble into a static, [equilibrium](/wiki/Chemical_equilibrium "Chemical equilibrium") end state. On the other hand, dynamic DNA nanotechnology focuses on complexes with useful non-equilibrium behavior such as the ability to reconfigure based on a chemical or physical stimulus. Some complexes, such as nucleic acid nanomechanical devices, combine features of both the structural and dynamic subfields.[[23]](#cite_note-DDNreview-23)[[24]](#cite_note-SDNreview-24)

The complexes constructed in structural DNA nanotechnology use topologically branched nucleic acid structures containing junctions. (In contrast, most biological DNA exists as an unbranched [double helix](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_double_helix "Nucleic acid double helix").) One of the simplest branched structures is a four-arm junction that consists of four individual DNA strands, portions of which are complementary in a specific pattern. Unlike in natural [Holliday junctions](/wiki/Holliday_junction "Holliday junction"), each arm in the artificial immobile four-arm junction has a different [base sequence](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_sequence "Nucleic acid sequence"), causing the junction point to be fixed at a certain position. Multiple junctions can be combined in the same complex, such as in the widely used double-crossover (DX) [structural motif](/wiki/Structural_motif "Structural motif"), which contains two parallel double helical domains with individual strands crossing between the domains at two crossover points. Each crossover point is, topologically, a four-arm junction, but is constrained to one orientation, in contrast to the flexible single four-arm junction, providing a rigidity that makes the DX motif suitable as a structural building block for larger DNA complexes.[[3]](#cite_note-Seeman-sciam-3)[[5]](#cite_note-Seeman2010-5)

Dynamic DNA nanotechnology uses a mechanism called [toehold-mediated strand displacement](/wiki/Toehold_mediated_strand_displacement "Toehold mediated strand displacement") to allow the nucleic acid complexes to reconfigure in response to the addition of a new nucleic acid strand. In this reaction, the incoming strand binds to a [single-stranded toehold region](/wiki/Sticky_end "Sticky end") of a double-stranded complex, and then displaces one of the strands bound in the original complex through a [branch migration](/wiki/Branch_migration "Branch migration") process. The overall effect is that one of the strands in the complex is replaced with another one.[[23]](#cite_note-DDNreview-23) In addition, reconfigurable structures and devices can be made using functional nucleic acids such as [deoxyribozymes](/wiki/Deoxyribozyme "Deoxyribozyme") and [ribozymes](/wiki/Ribozyme "Ribozyme"), which can perform chemical reactions, and [aptamers](/wiki/Aptamer "Aptamer"), which can bind to specific proteins or small molecules.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Structural DNA nanotechnology

Structural DNA nanotechnology, sometimes abbreviated as SDN, focuses on synthesizing and characterizing nucleic acid complexes and materials where the assembly has a static, equilibrium endpoint. The [nucleic acid double helix](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_double_helix "Nucleic acid double helix") has a robust, defined three-dimensional geometry that makes it possible to simulate,[[26]](#cite_note-Doye2013-26) predict and design the structures of more complicated nucleic acid complexes. Many such structures have been created, including two- and three-dimensional structures, and periodic, aperiodic, and discrete structures.[[24]](#cite_note-SDNreview-24)

### Extended lattices

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Mao-DXarray-schematic-small.gif/250px-Mao-DXarray-schematic-small.gif)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/DX_DNA_array.png/250px-DX_DNA_array.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/DNA_nanostructures.png/500px-DNA_nanostructures.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/SierpinskiTriangle.svg/250px-SierpinskiTriangle.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Rothemund-DNA-SierpinskiGasket.jpg/250px-Rothemund-DNA-SierpinskiGasket.jpg)

Small nucleic acid complexes can be equipped with [sticky ends](/wiki/Sticky_end "Sticky end") and combined into larger two-dimensional periodic lattices containing a specific [tessellated](/wiki/Tessellation "Tessellation") pattern of the individual molecular tiles.[[24]](#cite_note-SDNreview-24) The earliest example of this used double-crossover (DX) complexes as the basic tiles, each containing four sticky ends designed with sequences that caused the DX units to combine into periodic two-dimensional flat sheets that are essentially rigid two-dimensional crystals of DNA.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) Two-dimensional arrays have been made from other motifs as well, including the [Holliday junction](/wiki/Holliday_junction "Holliday junction") [rhombus](/wiki/Rhombus "Rhombus") lattice,[[32]](#cite_note-32) and various DX-based arrays making use of a double-cohesion scheme.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) The top two images at right show examples of tile-based periodic lattices.

Two-dimensional arrays can be made to exhibit aperiodic structures whose assembly implements a specific algorithm, exhibiting one form of DNA computing.[[17]](#cite_note-SeemanGrowing-17) The DX tiles can have their sticky end sequences chosen so that they act as [Wang tiles](/wiki/Wang_tile "Wang tile"), allowing them to perform computation. A DX array whose assembly encodes an [XOR](/wiki/Exclusive_or "Exclusive or") operation has been demonstrated; this allows the DNA array to implement a [cellular automaton](/wiki/Cellular_automaton "Cellular automaton") that generates a [fractal](/wiki/Fractal "Fractal") known as the [Sierpinski gasket](/wiki/Sierpinski_gasket "Sierpinski gasket"). The third image at right shows this type of array.[[29]](#cite_note-rothemund04winfree-29) Another system has the function of a binary [counter](/wiki/Counter_(digital) "Counter (digital)"), displaying a representation of increasing binary numbers as it grows. These results show that computation can be incorporated into the assembly of DNA arrays.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

DX arrays have been made to form hollow nanotubes 4–20 [nm](/wiki/Nanometer "Nanometer") in diameter, essentially two-dimensional lattices which curve back upon themselves.[[36]](#cite_note-Feldkamp-36) These DNA nanotubes are somewhat similar in size and shape to [carbon nanotubes](/wiki/Carbon_nanotube "Carbon nanotube"), and while they lack the electrical conductance of carbon nanotubes, DNA nanotubes are more easily modified and connected to other structures. One of many schemes for constructing DNA nanotubes uses a lattice of curved DX tiles that curls around itself and closes into a tube.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In an alternative method that allows the circumference to be specified in a simple, modular fashion using single-stranded tiles, the rigidity of the tube is an [emergent property](/wiki/Emergent_property "Emergent property").[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Forming three-dimensional lattices of DNA was the earliest goal of DNA nanotechnology, but this proved to be one of the most difficult to realize. Success using a motif based on the concept of [tensegrity](/wiki/Tensegrity "Tensegrity"), a balance between tension and compression forces, was finally reported in 2009.[[17]](#cite_note-SeemanGrowing-17)[[39]](#cite_note-3Darrays-39)

### Discrete structures

Researchers have synthesized many three-dimensional DNA complexes that each have the connectivity of a [polyhedron](/wiki/Polyhedron "Polyhedron"), such as a [cube](/wiki/Cube "Cube") or [octahedron](/wiki/Octahedron "Octahedron"), meaning that the DNA duplexes trace the [edges](/wiki/Edge_(geometry) "Edge (geometry)") of a polyhedron with a DNA junction at each vertex.[[6]](#cite_note-ShihYanChallenges-6) The earliest demonstrations of DNA polyhedra were very work-intensive, requiring multiple [ligations](/wiki/DNA_ligase "DNA ligase") and [solid-phase synthesis](/wiki/Solid-phase_synthesis "Solid-phase synthesis") steps to create [catenated](/wiki/Catenane "Catenane") polyhedra.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Subsequent work yielded polyhedra whose synthesis was much easier. These include a DNA octahedron made from a long single strand designed to fold into the correct conformation,[[41]](#cite_note-41) and a tetrahedron that can be produced from four DNA strands in one step, pictured at the top of this article.[[1]](#cite_note-Goodman05-1)

Nanostructures of arbitrary, non-regular shapes are usually made using the [DNA origami](/wiki/DNA_origami "DNA origami") method. These structures consist of a long, natural virus strand as a "scaffold", which is made to fold into the desired shape by computationally designed short "staple" strands. This method has the advantages of being easy to design, as the [base sequence](/wiki/Nucleic_acid_sequence "Nucleic acid sequence") is predetermined by the scaffold strand sequence, and not requiring high strand purity and accurate [stoichiometry](/wiki/Stoichiometry "Stoichiometry"), as most other DNA nanotechnology methods do. DNA origami was first demonstrated for two-dimensional shapes, such as a [smiley face](/wiki/Smiley "Smiley"), a coarse map of the Western Hemisphere, and the Mona Lisa painting.[[6]](#cite_note-ShihYanChallenges-6)[[13]](#cite_note-rothemundorigami-13)[[42]](#cite_note-42) Solid three-dimensional structures can be made by using parallel DNA helices arranged in a honeycomb pattern,[[14]](#cite_note-origami-14) and structures with two-dimensional faces can be made to fold into a hollow overall three-dimensional shape, akin to a cardboard box. These can be programmed to open and reveal or release a molecular cargo in response to a stimulus, making them potentially useful as programmable [molecular cages](/wiki/Molecular_encapsulation "Molecular encapsulation").[[43]](#cite_note-AndersenBox-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Templated assembly

Nucleic acid structures can be made to incorporate molecules other than nucleic acids, sometimes called heteroelements, including proteins, metallic nanoparticles, [quantum dots](/wiki/Quantum_dot "Quantum dot"), [amines](/wiki/Amine "Amine"),[[45]](#cite_note-45) and [fullerenes](/wiki/Fullerene "Fullerene"). This allows the construction of materials and devices with a range of functionalities much greater than is possible with nucleic acids alone. The goal is to use the self-assembly of the nucleic acid structures to template the assembly of the nanoparticles hosted on them, controlling their position and in some cases orientation.[[6]](#cite_note-ShihYanChallenges-6)[[46]](#cite_note-ChemicalReview-46)
Many of these schemes use a covalent attachment scheme, using oligonucleotides with [amide](/wiki/Amide "Amide") or [thiol](/wiki/Thiol "Thiol") functional groups as a chemical handle to bind the heteroelements. This covalent binding scheme has been used to arrange [gold nanoparticles](/wiki/Gold_nanoparticle "Gold nanoparticle") on a DX-based array,[[47]](#cite_note-47)
and to arrange [streptavidin](/wiki/Streptavidin "Streptavidin") protein molecules into specific patterns on a DX array.[[48]](#cite_note-48)
A non-covalent hosting scheme using [Dervan](/wiki/Peter_Dervan "Peter Dervan") polyamides on a DX array was used to arrange streptavidin proteins in a specific pattern on a DX array.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Carbon nanotubes have been hosted on DNA arrays in a pattern allowing the assembly to act as a [molecular electronic](/wiki/Molecular_scale_electronics "Molecular scale electronics") device, a [carbon nanotube field-effect transistor](/wiki/Carbon_nanotube_field-effect_transistor "Carbon nanotube field-effect transistor").[[50]](#cite_note-50) In addition, there are nucleic acid metallization methods, in which the nucleic acid is replaced by a metal which assumes the general shape of the original nucleic acid structure,[[51]](#cite_note-51) and schemes for using nucleic acid nanostructures as [lithography](/wiki/Lithography "Lithography") masks, transferring their pattern into a solid surface.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

## Dynamic DNA nanotechnology

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Strand_displacement.svg/250px-Strand_displacement.svg.png)

Dynamic DNA nanotechnology focuses on forming nucleic acid systems with designed dynamic functionalities related to their overall structures, such as computation and mechanical motion. There is some overlap between structural and dynamic DNA nanotechnology, as structures can be formed through annealing and then reconfigured dynamically, or can be made to form dynamically in the first place.[[6]](#cite_note-ShihYanChallenges-6)[[10]](#cite_note-nanomachines-10)

### Nanomechanical devices

DNA complexes have been made that change their conformation upon some stimulus, making them one form of [nanorobotics](/wiki/Nanorobotics "Nanorobotics"). These structures are initially formed in the same way as the static structures made in structural DNA nanotechnology, but are designed so that dynamic reconfiguration is possible after the initial assembly.[[23]](#cite_note-DDNreview-23)[[10]](#cite_note-nanomachines-10) The earliest such device made use of the transition between the [B-DNA](/wiki/B-DNA "B-DNA") and [Z-DNA](/wiki/Z-DNA "Z-DNA") forms to respond to a change in [buffer](/wiki/Buffer_solution "Buffer solution") conditions by undergoing a twisting motion.[[53]](#cite_note-53)
This reliance on buffer conditions caused all devices to change state at the same time. Subsequent systems could change states based upon the presence of control strands, allowing multiple devices to be independently operated in solution. Some examples of such s

enriched_points.expanded_content: DNA's unique properties include high specificity in base pairing, structural stability, and programmability, making it an ideal candidate for creating complex nanostructures.
Researchers can design DNA sequences tailored for specific functions, utilizing Watson-Crick base pairing to ensure precise assembly and stability in nanofabrication.
DNA nanostructures serve as effective drug delivery vehicles, enhancing the targeted release of therapeutic agents and improving treatment efficacy in various diseases.
DNA-based sensors can detect specific biomolecules with high sensitivity, enabling real-time monitoring of biological processes and facilitating early disease diagnosis.
Future research aims to enhance the stability, targeting ability, and multifunctionality of DNA nanostructures, paving the way for breakthroughs in nanomedicine and biotechnology.
